chunk_summary: This section discusses pediatric food allergy management through case-based vignettes, focusing on anaphylaxis, eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE), and associated psychosocial and nutritional complications.
learning_objectives: Understand the immediate and follow-up management of pediatric anaphylaxis, including the use of epinephrine and action plans.
Recognize the diagnostic approach and treatment options for eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE) in children and adolescents.
Identify and address psychosocial and nutritional complications, such as ARFID, in children with restrictive elimination diets.
Learn the importance of balancing symptom control with quality of life in managing food allergies and EoE.
key_facts: Anaphylaxis management includes IM epinephrine, a written action plan, and follow-up within 1–2 weeks.
EoE diagnosis requires endoscopy with biopsies; treatment options include PPI, swallowed steroids, or elimination diets.
Histological response, not just symptom improvement, is the gold standard for assessing EoE treatment efficacy.
Highly restrictive diets in EoE can lead to ARFID, weight loss, and social isolation, requiring multidisciplinary intervention.
School action plans for anaphylaxis should specify allergens, medication storage, and staff roles during meals and events.
reference_quote: "Anaphylaxis management includes IM epinephrine, a written action plan, and follow-up within 1–2 weeks." (Source: Anaphylaxis Management in OPD, IAP Action Plan 2026)
document_key_topics: Psychosocial and nutritional complications of elimination diets
